name: BURG / BURS Tree-Tiling Instruction Selection
summary: Fraser-Hanson-Proebsting 1992: instruction selection as tree-pattern matching on IR. Grammar rules with cost annotations; dynamic-programming matcher finds minimum-cost tiling. Linear-time finite-state tree automaton. Production case: MoarVM's expression JIT.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Fraser, Hanson, and Proebsting's **BURG** / **BURS** (Bottom-Up Rewrite Grammar / Bottom-Up Rewrite System, 1992) formulate instruction selection as tree-pattern matching on the IR: write instruction patterns as grammar rules with cost annotations, and a dynamic-programming matcher finds the minimum-cost tiling that covers the expression tree.

A rule looks like `Reg ← ADD(Reg, Reg) "add %0, %1, %2" cost 1`. Multiple rules can match the same IR node, and the matcher chooses the combination that minimises total cost. The generated selector is a finite-state tree automaton that runs in linear time in the IR size.

sections.title: Tree-tiling mechanism
sections.content:
Each grammar rule pairs an IR pattern (a tree shape) with a target instruction sequence and a cost. The matcher visits the IR tree bottom-up, computing for each node the minimum cost to reduce it to each non-terminal in the grammar. Once costs reach the root, a top-down pass extracts the cheapest cover, producing the chosen instructions.

BURG generators (`iburg`, `mburg`, `lburg`) compile the grammar plus costs into a target-specific selector at compile-build time. The runtime selector is small and fast: a few table lookups per IR node. This is one of the cleanest formulations of instruction selection in the literature.

sections.title: Limits and modern replacements
sections.content: BURG-style selection was dominant from the late 1980s through the mid-2000s. Its limitation is that it operates tree-locally — it cannot easily exploit instructions that span multiple IR nodes (like a fused multiply-add across a DAG). Modern compilers mostly moved to DAG- and CFG-level selection (LLVM's SelectionDAG, GlobalISel; Cranelift's ISLE), but tree tiling remains the clearest pedagogical model and is still used in small backends and generators like `iburg`.

sections.title: MoarVM expression JIT — production BURS
sections.content:
Raku's MoarVM uses a BURS-derived tiler as its primary instruction selector. Textual tile definitions specify a pattern, the function implementing the tile, the resulting symbol (used to match further tiles), and a cost. A per-architecture tile-table generator written in Perl precomputes the optimal-tile lookup table from the tile definitions, so that at JIT time selecting the best tile for an IR node reduces to a table lookup.

The generated tiler feeds into a linear-scan register allocator and DynASM-based code emission. The expression IR itself is a linear array with integer indices rather than pointers, for the same cache-locality reasons modern linear IRs (V8 Turboshaft, Cranelift CLIF) adopt.
